ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fried shallots as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for garnish-level food items is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried shallots used as a garnish is approximately 5 g (about 1 teaspoon), sprinkled over congee, noodle soup, or stir-fried dishes.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Shallots, raw (FDC ID 11677) and Onions, dehydrated flakes (FDC ID 11284), adapted for deep-fried shallot preparation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for deep-fried shallots exists in USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy shallot (raw) and dehydrated onion flake entries, adjusted for moisture loss and oil absorption during deep-frying. Energy, fat, and carbohydrate values reflect the concentrated, oil-absorbed state of the fried product. Values are cross-referenced against FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Southeast Asian fried shallot preparations. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with this adaptation and have been retained with minor rounding.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Allium Vegetables and Fried Preparations — analogous estimate based on shallot/onion GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for deep-fried shallots is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy with raw and cooked onion/shallot entries (GI range 10–20, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The deep-frying process adds fat, which further slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption, supporting a GI at the lower end of the onion/shallot range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate per serving in g) / 100 = (15 × 1.625) / 100 ≈ 0.24, rounded to 0.2. As a garnish used in 5 g portions, the glycemic impact is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (10 mg per 100 g; 0.5 mg per 5 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Fried shallots are naturally very low in sodium and are safe for individuals managing hypertension, provided no additional salt is added during preparation; quercetin and organosulfur compounds in shallots may additionally support vascular health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI ~15) and negligible carbohydrate load per typical garnish serving (approximately 1.6 g carbohydrate per 5 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: When used as a garnish in standard 5 g portions, fried shallots contribute a glycemic load of approximately 0.2 and are unlikely to meaningfully raise postprandial blood glucose; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may use them freely as a flavoring agent, though the high fat content (45 g per 100 g) warrants portion awareness in the context of overall caloric intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat content (45 g per 100 g) from deep-frying oil absorption, with moderate saturated fat (approximately 6.5 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the small garnish serving size (5 g) limits absolute fat intake to approximately 2.25 g per serving, individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of cumulative oil intake from fried garnishes; the quercetin and flavonoid content of shallots may offer modest cardioprotective antioxidant benefits, but the quality and type of frying oil used is an important consideration.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Organosulfur compounds and quercetin with anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Shallots contain organosulfur compounds and flavonoids such as quercetin with documented anti-inflammatory properties; however, repeated use of degraded or reused frying oil may introduce pro-inflammatory oxidized lipids, so freshly prepared fried shallots using high-quality oil are preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Shallots (炸红葱头, Bawang Goreng) are a ubiquitous garnish and flavoring agent in Chinese, Indonesian, Malaysian, and broader Southeast Asian cuisines. They are produced by thinly slicing small red shallots (Allium ascalonicum) and deep-frying them in vegetable oil until they reach a golden-brown, crispy state. The deep-frying process drives off most of the moisture, concentrating the shallots' natural sugars and sulfur compounds, which transforms the raw, pungent allium into sweet, savory, and intensely aromatic flakes with a satisfying crunch. Per 100 g, fried shallots are energy-dense (approximately 550 kcal) due to oil absorption during frying, with a macronutrient profile of approximately 45 g fat, 32.5 g carbohydrate, and 4.5 g protein. Dietary fibre is relatively high at 5.5 g per 100 g. Sodium content is naturally very low at 10 mg per 100 g, making them suitable for low-sodium diets. They are a good source of potassium (450 mg), folate (65 mcg), and vitamin E (4.5 mg). The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), and the glycemic load per standard 5 g garnish serving is negligible (approximately 0.2). Clinically, fried shallots are low-concern for hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes when used in typical garnish quantities. Their high fat content warrants moderate awareness for individuals managing cardiovascular disease or following low-fat dietary regimens. Shallots are rich in quercetin and organosulfur compounds, which have documented antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. In Southeast Asian Chinese households, both the crispy shallots and the fragrant shallot oil produced during frying are retained as kitchen staples. Common uses include garnishing congee (rice porridge), noodle soups, dry-tossed noodles, stir-fried vegetables, and steamed fish. They are also known as Bawang Goreng in Malay and Indonesian, and as 炸干葱 or 炸红葱头 in Chinese.
common_user_queries: How many calories in bawang goreng?
What is the GI of fried shallots?

What is 炸红葱头 in English?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for deep-fried shallots exists in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as a standalone product. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy shallot and dehydrated onion flake entries, adjusted for moisture loss and oil absorption during deep-frying. The input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with this adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney onion/shallot data. Overall confidence is rated medium due to the absence of a direct approved-source match for the fried preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for raw shallots (FDC ID 11677) and dehydrated onion flakes (FDC ID 11284), adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption and moisture loss. No exact approved-source entry for commercially or traditionally prepared fried shallots was identified. 2. Saturated fat (6.5 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (18.0 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (18.0 g) are estimated based on typical vegetable oil fatty acid profiles used in Southeast Asian frying; these values carry lower confidence than core macros. 3. Trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 g, consistent with the use of non-hydrogenated vegetable oil; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central fatty acid data for vegetable oils. 4. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae (0.0 mcg RAE), Vitamin_d_mcg (0.0 mcg), and Vitamin_b12_mcg (0.0 mcg) are reported as 0.0: shallots are naturally zero in vitamin D and B12; vitamin A content in shallots is negligible and rounds to zero per USDA data. These are confirmed zero or naturally zero values. 5. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 mg: fried shallots are a plant-based food and naturally contain no cholesterol; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 6. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database onion/shallot entries (GI range 10–20); no direct GI measurement for fried shallots was found. 7. Glycemic load per serving = (15 × 1.625) / 100 ≈ 0.24, rounded to 0.2 for a 5 g serving. 8. The portions array in the input contained three identical duplicate entries (all 5 g); only one canonical serving of 5 g is retained. 9. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are best-available matches; reviewers should verify against current FoodOn and FoodEx2 release versions.
